mapping.a_element: the extreme value (maximum of N iid/weakly-correlated variables) whose distribution is studied
mapping.b_element: the maximal height of N non-intersecting Brownian excursions / largest eigenvalue of a random matrix

mapping.a_element: the limit distribution of the extreme (Gumbel/Frechet/Weibull iid universality classes)
mapping.b_element: the Tracy-Widom limit distribution (strongly-correlated universality class)

mapping.a_element: finite size N of the data set and its scaling
mapping.b_element: system size L / number of walkers N in the large-N limit

mapping.a_element: finite-size convergence exponent setting the shape-correction function
mapping.b_element: scaling near the critical point L_c ~ sqrt(N) of the third-order phase transition

brief_justification: Both study the limiting distribution of a maximum and its scaling with system size, but the underlying machinery differs: A applies a renormalization method to classify iid/weakly-correlated extreme-value universality classes (Gumbel-type) via finite-size shape corrections, whereas B relies on an exact mapping of reunion probabilities to Yang-Mills/random-matrix partition functions that produces a third-order phase transition and the Tracy-Widom law -- a distinct strongly-correlated EVS class that A's iid machinery does not generate. The shared element is the object (an extremal distribution) and the goal (system-size scaling), not the governing mechanism.
